comment_content: The course taught discrete math in different categories like permutations and logic. Some of them are really interesting like crypto. A fun class for me.
comment_teaching: Prof. Sunil teaches explains everything clearly and likes to interact with students. The only thing is that he teaches a little slow. But other than that, I love his classes.
comment_workload: There are only 4 homework through out the semester, which makes its workload light. But the homework are a little difficult.
comment_grading: I didn't really spend much time on this course (comparing to math 1023) and got full marks on all exams and homework. And of course received an A+.

comment_content: The content this course covers goes far beyond simply discrete math. It mentions some important computer-science-related logics such as the mathematics beneath recursion, and some parts of statistics also work well with its topic. The only thing to regret is that the part about graphs is barely discussed in the term for a limited number of class hours. Overall, the content is very helpful and meaningful for computer science students. (But for dear JUPAS students, a prerequisite of M1/M2 does not mean M1/M2 works in this course! ;_;)
comment_teaching: Arya is a super exprienced lecturer able to demonstrate complicated and counter-natural discrete math concepts in a way that's far less boring. I can still recall some jokes he made (though those were very discrete-math-specific and engineering-style jokes so not many laughed in class :D). Most proof and equation are established together with we students step by step, by which it provides a lot of insights. Additionally, it is very possible to see that Arya knows the content very well.
comment_workload: The workload is generally low. The only compulsory things else than exams leave only 4 assignments, each of around 13 questions. Well, half of the questions could be very difficult, but after all with some help you can easily get them done. Major workload comes from brain power consumed during lessons and drawn for revision.
comment_grading: The grading of this course as an HONOR COURSE is a bit harsh: to be more exact, the final A range cut-off is somehow similar to the normal version COMP2711 or even higher this semester, while the paper itself turns out to be of a certain level of difficulty, it is not easy to get a good grade compared with other honor courses. But fair enough, this is caused mainly due to too many discrete math masters taking their place this semester to make it a hard game mode, meaning that the culprit may not be the course itself. For reference, my two exams are like mean+1sd and mean+1.5sd and I missed an assignment, then I got a B+. If you would like challenges, 2711H is good for you; or otherwise, go with COMP2711.

comment_content:
The content is very easy. I think for the mainland students, they need not put any efforts in the first half semester because most of these knowledge has been covered in high school. (especially you have take math competition during high school). For the second half of semester, the random variable part is interesting (and not such easy), also for the graph part.

However, the tutorial problem is not such easy. And I really like the tutorial problem. Some of them are hard and can encourage me to think and study the theory behind the problem.
comment_teaching:
I have to say that ARYA, Sunil is one of the best professor I have met in HKUST. I can hear his enthusiasm from his voice. He tries to use daily example to help us understand the abstract concept. He also leads us break down the tedious proof step by step, trying to let us observe the spark inside proof and then reach the aha moment.

Also, for the TA, Sergei Novozhilov, I think his teaching might not be excellent, but I can observe his efforts in his tutorial problem (just like what I mentioned before).
comment_workload: The workload is quite small. You only have 4 homework. Though some questions are hard, you have plenty of time to finish it.
comment_grading:
The exam is very easy.
Actually, in our midterm, mode is full mark (100). Interesting, right? So, during the lecture, professor tells us that the final exam would be harder. However, the mean for final is 75.63, and upper quality is 90.3, which is still very high (comparing to other COMP course).
And the professor generously gives many A+ I think. Because even though I haven't got full mark in both exam and homework, I still receive an A+.

comment_content:
The content of the course:

Predicate Logic
Rules of Inference
Modular Arithmetic
GCDs
Modular Multiplicative Inverses
Chinese Remainder Theorem
Cryptography
Asymptotic Notations
Probabilities
Binomial Coefficients, Polynomials and Combinatorics
Random Variables, Expectations: Definitions and Properties, Applications
Variance;
Inducion and Recursion
Graphs (were covered partially)

The set of topics is good and appropriate for those students who do not have a solid background in the field of computer science. Otherwise, one may find the content oversimplified and scarce. The content of lectures is stuck to the Rosen's textbook. Tutorials are helpful since the tasks provided are similar to those on the examinations.
comment_teaching:
Prof. Arya is very kind and understanding. In addition, the lectures are well-designed for those who find even basic proofs scary or tedious.
TAs provide a wide range of the tasks and their solutions that helps to grasp the foundations well.

If you are not a CS student, but want to pursue a major in DSCT, this course fits you well. Otherwise, you may have another opinion about the course.
comment_workload:
Workload is quite heavy. Homeworks are immense and took me 1-3 days to finish, the techniques and theorems may demand a lot of time to digest.

It is clearly aimed at students without a strong background in informatics or mathematics competitions: if you are just a student who got A- or above in your MATH1014 course, workload will be high due to the homeworks and the trade-off mentioned.

Despite the fact that your background affect your performance significantly, the main purpose of the course is to teach you solve basic mathematical problems applied for computer science. So, since they are not really overloaded with the concepts, the key factor affecting your grade is your accuracy since every single mistake is costly in terms of scores and penalties are generally harsh.
comment_grading:
Grading scheme for the term:

20% Assignments
35% Midterm
45% Final

Examinations include both proof-based questions and calculations regarding the topics mentioned above. Proof-based questions are not complicated as long as you are familiar with the content and structure your proofs in a precise and appropriate manner. Students should review all the tutorials, past papers and PPTs before the exam - and it will be fine. In addition, homeworks are designed for the preparation to the exams since the basic solution techniques are the same for both HW and exams.

Despite the description in the syllabus, letter grades are assigned according to the curve. The grading policy is a trade-off. On the one hand, A-range is approximately 55-60%. On the other hand, means and medians for the course are high (roughly about 80 and 85, respectively).

Therefore, if you do not have any profound understanding of the concepts before the course, you might well get A- if you put in some effort and B+ without too much effort because B+ range is also wide. However, the competition for solid A and A+ is enormously fierce since the vast majority of the students are those who are familiar with the concepts well like informatics or math Olympiad participants, even IOI/IMO.

comment_content: The content is genuinely interesting and v different from you what have learnt in your high school and board exams. However, imo, to completely grasp the concepts, a bit more time than 4 months would be more appropriate as such to enjoy the stuff rather than just skimming.
comment_teaching: Can't say anything coz I only attended 1 class. But one thing bout prof Arya which can  be taken as both pos and neg depending on you is that he is v slow paced and would get excited over simplest things; so if you are impatient like me, skipping classes and studying on your own would be your best bet
comment_workload: Apart from 4 hws and self study, there is not much to it. J ensure you do past papers and hws on your own. Can skip tutorials as the questions are diabolical.
comment_grading:
Generous....

Got mean in mid terms; cooked finals thou

Ended up with A

comment_content:
The contents are similar to COMP 2711 (non-honor version), but Professor Sunil would provide a little bit of extra knowledges (not much but you cannot find them in the notes). For short, they're basically the same.
For those who wonder the syllabus:
1. Logics
2. Set Theory
3. Number theory (with RSA cryptography)
4. Algorithm notation (big-O, big-Omega, big-Theta)
5. Counting and Probability
6. Recursion
7. Induction
8. Graph Theory
Basically the same as COMP2711.
comment_teaching: Professor Sunil has a lot of passion towards teaching, his classes are not boring. In the classes, he shows every steps clearly and even the thinking process. The lectures are different from the notes, but still the same content just the order. It is very recommended to attend his lectures even you are not enrolled into honor version. You can definitely gain deeper knowledge via his classes.
comment_workload:
4 assignments in total. Biweekly assignments, with difficulty higher than exams. The questions are relatively fun, and I consider them as more difficult than normal version. Some skills may be applied in the exam paper, but won't be too difficult as professor said just want to test if we have a clear concept, not we can derive a very difficult things.

For tutorials, the questions' difficulty is diabolical. Luckily, tutorial things are not scored, or else it would be a disaster. The tutorial questions are very difficult, but of course still fun to attempt. The TAs are good and able to explain them quite well, which you can learn a lot from the tutorials. Although tutorial skills have a high chance that they wouldn't appear in the exam paper, still worthy to try as the questions are very interesting.
comment_grading: The grading is very generous. I scored around LQ (tho LQ is likely to be around 70-80), usually I would be graded as C range if in other courses. In this course, I got B+ as my final grade. The papers are relatively not difficult, as long as you are able to follow the class, or even able to do 50% of homework, you will be fine. Furthermore, I think the A-range is about 50%, which means median would be A-.

comment_content:

comment_teaching:
Arya is the reason I come to ust instead of skipping lectures because he teaches concepts super clearly, so I don't have to read the slides again to understand the content. He is also very enthusiastic and passionate, and his lessons are quite engaging. The best part about him is that he tries to present proofs insightfully. For example, he uses Euler's totient function to show how someone can come up with RSA cryptography instead of just restating the ppt proof.

Tbh tho if you're a fast learner or have previously learnt the materials in primary school then Arya's style might not suit you because his pace is quite slow for an honors course. He also teaches with intuition in mind so you might feel like he is treating you like a kid if you're an analytical guy.
comment_workload:
Just 4 easy assignments every 2 weeks cus if you're going into honors these should be a walk in the park.

Just kidding assignments are difficult but they are still kind of doable. If you have no clue how to do it feel free to consult AI for help, but it's better to do it yourself because once you see the solution it's really obvious. You'll still do fine if you use AI though because Arya's midterm and final questions don't require many "tricks" to solve.

Apart from that it's just the workload from preparing midterms and finals.
comment_grading:
Somehow the papers are easier than comp2711 in the same semester, but the grading can be strict as they require you to justify almost everything. In fact, they can even give you full marks if your logic is correct but you made careless mistakes midway. However, if you forgot to explain how you got to a certain equation or inequality (e.g., writing down 1+(n/(n+1))<=2 without explanation), then they will deduct marks.

comment_content: This course will cover topics from very diverse fields in a substantial way. Most of the time, you'll only learn about the fundamentals of each topic due to the time constraints and the huge number of topics. Don't be fooled by the COMP in the course code; this course covers MATH subjects that you're going to need for your COMP courses (e.g., modular arithmetic for cryptography and security). The content is fun, but it's not substantially different from 2711's content (apparently, this heavily depends on the instructor). I would've loved to dive deeper into some of the topics. I must admit that I expected much more from an honors course.
comment_teaching: ARYA is clearly passionate about the subjects he teaches. I was familiar with some of the course content beforehand, yet I still attended his lectures solely because of his teaching style. His style can make even the most boring topics enjoyable. He'll emphasize the right spots, engage with the class, and encourage the students to see the bigger picture.
comment_workload: Throughout the course, you'll only need to complete 4 assignments. These assignments typically include around 10 questions. Most of the problems are intuitive and approachable, with only a few being quite challenging. I would suggest paying extra attention to homework problems, as ARYA likes to pull questions from the homework and past midterms. You'll get 2 weeks for each assignment, which should be way more than enough.
comment_grading:
ARYA has probably one of the best gradings in UST. This year's grading scheme is:
Assignments 20%
Mid-Term Exam 35%
Final Exam 45%

The statistics for the midterm:
Mean: 80.01
Standard deviation: 17.79
Max: 100 (10 students)
Median: 85

The statistics for the final: N/A

For the homework, only some of the problems are graded. They don't specify which problems are going to be graded beforehand, so you need to solve all of the problems for a riskless grade.

comment_content:
Compared to COMP2711, the content is actually not a lot harder, but relatively focused more on theory. However, there's no video for the lectures, so you do need to take notes to avoid missing any important stuff.
The tutorial is really hard for me and It takes me a lot of time to solve the tutorial problem set. But the exam questions are way easier.

Sunil delivers almost the best teaching in CSE department, you must be able to understand the concept if you really pay attention to lectures.
His lessons are very engaging, and he is very enthusiastic in teaching so it just makes the concept easier to understand.
comment_workload: Unless you try to solve all the tutorial questions, this course would not take you that much time, instead, doing some in-book exercises is sufficient.
comment_grading:

So, it is just matter of carefulness, if you consider yourself a careful person, I think you can get A range in this course.

comment_content: Less than high school math Olympiad number theory and combinatorics , but have some intro to crypto, induction, recursion, algorithm, graph theory, quite light for people with some proof experience, as a good bridge for real analysis in terms of building up math maturity for math majors as well. Basically the same as 2711 but we have a much better professor here (his UST ranking is top 5)
comment_teaching:
Prof Arya’s teaching is great, really passionate and cares about math beauty (especially in combinatorial proofs), despite previewing ppt before each lecture, he always manages to say something I never thought of, and he tells a lot of jokes, (one time accidentally say “what the f…” and took it back hahahah) Also, he and the TAs reply to emails fast.

Update: His teaching gets better as the stuff gets harder. After mid terms, we started combinatorics and probability. I actually learnt the probability properly in here but not in ISOM 2500 (business statistics) which I learnt nothing there. Also, he asks us to first make a guess, and then do the math rigorously so ensure that we are thinking on the right track, this mindset is really good and makes us do maths more mindfully, really really appreciate his teaching!!

Update (towards the end of sem): Prof Arya didn’t manage to finish all of graph theory. As set theory was excluded as well (but the concepts related to injection and surjection are included in combinatorics), we can see that 2711H covers less materials than 2711, but has more in depth analysis of other key topics.

(Update): on the last day of the course he asked if the materials are too difficult, but then he said but it’s too late to drop the course. What a good way to end the course. Hahaha
comment_workload:
2 assignments before mt and 2 before finals, released 14 days before submission and you know exactly when they will be released in the course syllabus.
Before mt, as I have some prior knowledge, I usually finish the assignments within 2 days after releasing, so the workload is light for me, but things are getting much harder after mid term, so wish me luck.

(Update): hw 3,4 are wild. I have no idea about half of the questions and I’m so cooked. It took me 5 days to finish each of them, and the solution is amazingly simple and elegant. (Btw, it is usually the case that you can finish one problem with half a page, if you’re writing pages of calculation,  you’re definitely on the wrong track, or at least, not the optimal track) Also, the assignments perfectly complement the materials from the lecture and extend it a little bit, I can feel the professors passion about utilising every opportunity to help us learn more and deeper.
comment_grading:
Mid term:
Mean: 80.71, sd: 17.2, median: 85, UQ: 97, mode: 100, yes, 10 people out of 90 got 100 marks. It’s insane, I shouldn’t be here.
The grading of questions are really really lenient, try to write some definitions out even if you have entirely no clue. I was one step away from finishing the last question, but they regarded my answer to be totally correct.

Before finals, Prof Arya announced that the final would be super hard as we performed too well in mid terms (he wanted the mean to be 70 but we got 80, so he would make the final mean at around 55-60). Actually quite scared, let’s see what happens.

Final exam: mean 75.68, sd 20.68, median 80, UQ 90.5.
However, despite Prof Arya saying it would be difficult, the final exam was very easy, I’d say much easier than hw, I struggled a lot with the assignments, and after struggling, you’d find the exam paper very easy. However, please make sure to understand the tricks from the assignment as they will appear exactly in your final, and he even have us doing it twice in the last 2 Qs. This year, 2711H paper is way easier than that of 2711. There’s really no reason not to take this.

2711 is 2.5h for 11Q and 2711H is 3h for 8Q. Also, we were not tested on materials before mt at all but 2711 did. We didn’t have graph at all but 2711 did. So now you understand the difficulty of 2711H, plus 50% A range, is there any reason not to take this?

My score:
Assignment (20%): 20/20 (mean is around 18)
Mid term (35%): 97/100 (I forgot the proof of gcd)
Final (45%): 100/100 (really easy if you understand all pp and hw)
Total: 98.95%
Grade: A+ (I definitely cannot get A+ in regular 2711)

Please note that they care a lot about how you present your proofs/steps. Even for non-proof questions, it’s the safest to write down all the steps and formulae with justification, and explain how you got each step to the next step with a very short sentence or a bracket next to the steps to indicate some properties. As for algebraic steps, they deduct marks if you skip a lot of them as they think you may not know how things cancel out or combine, you just see the final answer (proof questions) and pretend you did it

comment_content:
Logic: propositional logic, predicate logic, rules of inference, proof techniques
Set Theory: sets, functions
Number Theory: cryptography and modular arithmetic, Euclid’s division theorem, multiplicative inverse, divisibility, RSA cryptosystem, Chinese remainder theorem
Counting: pigeonhole principle, basic and generalized permutations and combinations, binomial coefficients, inclusion-exclusion principle
Probability: probability theory, Bayes theorem, expectation, variance, random variables
Induction & Recursion: mathematical induction, strong induction and well-ordering property, recursion, recurrence relations
Graph: graph representation, isomorphism, connectivity, Eulerian paths, Hamiltonian paths, planarity, graph coloring (not tested in final exam)

comment_teaching:
I really like the way Amir teaches. His lectures start with understanding a problem and then go on to solving it, so that knowledge can be presented in a form suitable for entering the brain. He gave almost every theorem understandable proof, and the matching examples are sufficient, multi-angle and not excessive.
So I enjoyed his class.

As for the formal assignments, they should be handed in latex, which I think is good practice of latex ability.
I also like the process of doing homework. Difficult questions can get students to discuss spontaneously.
A wrong question got me thinking for a long time, but it wasn't that necessary.

At first I was still working on the Problem Sets, and then I stopped working on it. I fell behind about 5, but I almost made up for the final week.
TA’s lectures are not as good as Amir, but at least their academic ability is good. Later, I found that the answers they wrote were also quite convenient to read.

Exams are fun. It brings together the content of the lecture in a way I never thought possible.

Through this course, I found that learning professional knowledge can be as interesting as listening to talk shows and playing games to fight monsters. Thanks Amir for not only bringing me knowledge, but also bringing me interest.
comment_workload: I think wl is flexible. Those who want to learn more can do homework and read more textbooks.
comment_grading: The grading is good.

comment_workload:
你说的对，但是2711H是一门由Amir自主开发的全新开放思维冒险课程。课程开设在一个被称作HKUST的幻想世界，在这里，人们将被要求引导算法之力，完成小学奥数。你将扮演一位名为「Computer Scientist」的神秘角色，在自由的学习中邂逅不会计算、但一起绽放笑容的同伴们，和他们一起找回年少自信。（听说学2711H的同学都天生爱笑：）
毫不夸张地说，2711H是一门为大一新生量身定做，被大二学长强力推荐，零门槛，难度适中，给龟easy，考试人性化的优秀课程。Amir随性幽默专业的谈吐，更让学习变成一种享受。经常上2711H的同学大多眼神锐利，极度自信，且智力逐渐增加。上2711H会重组大脑结构，让小脑变为大脑，最后完全变成「Computer Scientist」。上2711H的学生具有强烈的数感，人一旦开始爱上2711H，就说明这个人gpa通通都是上等，这辈子肯定在UST大杀四方，对世界格局起到决定性的作用。
一个不喜欢2711H的人，无非只能有两种可能性。一种是不认为省队竞赛题、IMO试题与小猿口算难度相当的人。因为自己没有能力抽出一点时间去读每周几本书的课余小任务等自身因素，而把做不出题归咎于题目难度，他们的人生是失败的。如果你学三周2711H却不能拿信竞金牌，只能说明你没有思考足够长时间的week problem set；如果你学三周2711H没能参加IMOCMOUSAMO并拔得头筹，只能说明你对Amir的recommend book list视而不见。第二种可能：没有感受到Amir个人魅力的人。那么这个人的思想境界便低到了一个令人发指的程度。一个人不喜欢Amir，只能证明此人个人品味之低下，是阴暗的，是扭曲的，是无法融入社会的。Amir怎么你了，我每天18小时观看Amir课程回放，每节课能学到2到3年世界前列的数学研究成果。我半节课学完逻辑门，1天能熟练使用latex，2节课掌握数归良序递降，3周精通图论数论，我一门语言都不会，却能轻松理解DFS、BFS、动态规划以及各种NP完全问题，虽然我只读大一，但已经超越了中国绝大多数人（包括你）的水平，换算过来最少也是Yau的徒弟了，这便是我骄傲的资本。这恰好说明了2711H课程使学生体现出来的谦虚和自信，这也造就了这个课程学生互帮互助的局面。Amir负责课上输出优质内容，学生负责课后形成良好的学习氛围，如此良好的课程模式令那些绞尽脑汁却找不到人听课的教授艳羡不已，令lang课group discussion只能听到2711H讨论声的老师自惭形秽。可以说，选课不选2711H，就像四大名著不读红楼梦，说明对于数学造诣和自我修养不足，理解不了算法与哲学统一魅力的高雅艺术，它只能看到外表的数字和计算，参不透计算机异或内核，只能度过一个相对失败的人生。

comment_content:
If you are trying to read this review to get a hang of how the course is like and whether you should take it, I suggest you leave. And do not read any of the other reviews on fall 2024-25, because Amir has already left ust and you will not be able to take this version of 2711H ever again.

Moving on to the content part, please read the other reviews of the same semester to get a hang of what was taught. I don't know if any of you have ever came in contact with olympiad mathematics. Questions you see in this course is extremely similar to them. I have came in contact with them when I was in primary school so I think I handled it better than some people in my class.
comment_teaching:
Amir is a walking aura emitter. His teaching is one of its kind. I have never seen anyone in my life teach at the same level as him. He has the ability to explain a difficult concept in a few minutes with extremely precise language. He is like Desmond, but he teaches at 3 times his speed. How he does it is by presenting his logic flow in an ordered manner, splitting each problem into smaller problems, and solving each sub problems to prove the actual question.

I also like his approach of teaching. He basically just gives us problems in lessons, explains how to do it, and move on to the next problem. This way, the content is easier to understand and you also have the opportunity to think on your own before seeing the answer.

I also feel like what I learned from him is more than knowledge. It's a mindset. It's an approach to difficult discrete mathematics problems. Whenever I struggle in some problems, I try to think what Amir would do. I would rewatch his videos and learn the way he proves. At some point, I just realised the method of proofing became part of my toolbox. If the hammer doesn't work, I will try using the screwdriver. I will keep testing until it works, and it really sped up my problem solving process as I no longer have to sit in front of the problem and wonder how I am suppose to solve it.

By the way, please check out his youtube channel if you want to have a taste of what his teaching is like or want to pre study 2711H. It's very challenging but worth watching. I think the only part he didn't teach well was number theory. The rest of them were all very good.
comment_workload:
The workload is supposed to be E. The reason I gave it an A is because I didn't want to mess up the overall rating of the course too badly as I know this version of 2711H is not gonna appear anymore.

As the other reviews had mentioned, there are 10 books amir told us to read, additional videos each week to watch and problem sets every week. The workload is not for humans. I could study this course alone in the whole semester and I wouldn't be able to finish all the work he gave us. So I basically gave up on doing any of them after the fifth week and hope that everyone else is doing the same. I think Amir gave us so much exercise and resources because that's how he studied when he was a student? I am just guessing though. Anyway Amir had high expectations of us and we failed to meet it.

comment_content:
First of all, Amir will leave ust after this term. So, the review is just for fun.

The course will cover the following material:

Proofs and Reasoning: Propositional Logic, First-order (Predicate) Logic, Quantifiers, Proof by contradiction, Mathematical Induction, the Pigeonhole Principle, the Invariant Principle, the Extremal Principle, ...
Set Theory: ZMC, Peano's Axioms, Dedekind Cuts, Relations, Functions, Ordinals and Cardinals, Russel's Paradox, ...
Enumerative Combinatorics: Rules of Sum and Product, Permutations and Combinations, Inclusion-Exclusion, Catalan and Stirling Numbers, Generating Functions, Recurrence Relations, Double Counting, ...
Probability Theory: Discrete and Countable Probability Spaces, Axiomatic Probability Theory, Measure Functions, Lebesgue Integrals, Conditional Probability, Independence, Random Variables, Bayesian Reasoning, Expectation, Stochastic Processes, Markov Chains, Markov Decision Processes, ...
Number Theory: Divisibility, GCD, LCM, Primes, Congruence, Chinese Remainder Theorem, Theorems of Fermat, Euler and Wilson, Quadratic Reciprocity Law, the RSA and El-Gamal Cryptosystems, ...
Graph Theory: Adjacency, Walks, Paths, Cycles, (Strong) Connectivity, Distance, Trees, Spanning Trees, Bipartite Graphs, Matchings, Shortest Paths, ...
Game Theory: Combinatorial Games, Sprague-Grundy Theorem, Normal Form (Nash) Games, Nash Equilibria, Correlated Equilibria, Infinite Games on Graphs, ...

You see sooooooooooooooo many content. And many topics are about graphs (graph theory, game theory markov chain...). Amir really loves graphs. So you survive if you understand those graphs.

If you are curious about what Amir looks like or how he teaches, search him on YouTube and you'll see his channel with lecture recordings.

comment_content:
Reminder that this course in the future is not going to be as hard as this term. (Unless there is an even "crazier" professor than Amir which is very very unlikely.) This course was really something special. A lot of things have covered this term. It was fun definitely. I love math after all. But to master each topic in a week and train hard. That made my enjoyment throughout the course decrease day by day, week by week. The course this term has a lot more connection to higher level math courses (Real Analysis, Combinatorial Analysis, Game Theory, etc. just look at the syllabus and you will understand.)

Here is the syllabus and after that there will be the schedule for the whole course.

Syllabus:
    Proofs and Reasoning: Propositional Logic, First-order (Predicate) Logic, Quantifiers, Proof by contradiction, Mathematical Induction, the Pigeonhole Principle, the Invariant Principle, the Extremal Principle, ...
    Set Theory: ZMC, Peano's Axioms, Dedekind Cuts, Relations, Functions, Ordinals and Cardinals, Russel's Paradox, ...
    Enumerative Combinatorics: Rules of Sum and Product, Permutations and Combinations, Inclusion-Exclusion, Catalan and Stirling Numbers, Generating Functions, Recurrence Relations, Double Counting, ...
    Probability Theory: Discrete and Countable Probability Spaces, Axiomatic Probability Theory, Measure Functions, Lebesgue Integrals, Conditional Probability, Independence, Random Variables, Bayesian Reasoning, Expectation, Stochastic Processes, Markov Chains, Markov Decision Processes, ...
    Number Theory: Divisibility, GCD, LCM, Primes, Congruence, Chinese Remainder Theorem, Theorems of Fermat, Euler and Wilson, Quadratic Reciprocity Law, the RSA and El-Gamal Cryptosystems, ...
    Graph Theory: Adjacency, Walks, Paths, Cycles, (Strong) Connectivity, Distance, Trees, Spanning Trees, Bipartite Graphs, Matchings, Shortest Paths, ...
    Game Theory: Combinatorial Games, Sprague-Grundy Theorem, Normal Form (Nash) Games, Nash Equilibria, Correlated Equilibria, Infinite Games on Graphs, ...

Some of them are not covered by Amir, but you are required to read the books to learn it.

Here is how the flow so far according to how Amir teaches (From first lecture to latest lectures):

Logic and Proofs: Propositional Logic, Predicate, Quantifiers, definitions of natural numbers ,Peano's Axioms , Induction, Well-ording , Infinite Descent , introduction to Pigeonhole Principle, Strong Induction, Extremal Principle.

Combinatorics in general: Counting, Permutation, Combination with and without repetitions, Principle of Inclusion and Exclusion. More about Pigeonhole Principle.

Big focus on Graph Theory: theorems for graphs, isomorphism , path, walks, connected graphs, connected components, trees, cycles, algorithms on creating trees, bipartite, eulerian circuit, Hamlitonian cycle/graphs, graphical graphs , Havel Hakimi Algorithm ,Minimum Spanning Trees, Kruskal's algorithm, Prim's Algorithm , directed graph, DAG (Directed Acyclic Graph), Topological Ordering, DFS, BFS algorithms, Huffman Coding algorithm, eccentricity, shortest path, dijkstra algorithm, matchings, Hall's marriage theorem,König-Egeváry theorem, Gallai Theorem , vertex covers, edge covers, independent sets, network flow, ford-fulkerson algorithm, stable matching and graph coloring.

Number Theory: extension to Peano axiom to integers rather than natural number,definition of negative numbers, definiton of operations on integers, absolute value,divisibility, gcd, relatively prime/coprime, Euclidean algorithm, lcm, prime factorization, fundamental of arithmetic Congruence, Modular Multiplicative Inverse, Chinese Remainder Theorem, Fermat Little Theorem, Euler Theorem, Wilson's theorem, Diffie-Hellman-Merkle Key Exchange (Symmetric key), Elgamal encryption.

Set Theory: Naive Comprehension/Naive Set Theory, Russell's Paradox, Defining language for Set Theory, ZFC 10 Axioms (Extensionality, Empty Set, Unordered pair, Union, Comprehension, Powerset, Infinity, Replacement, Foundation, Axiom of Choice (there are 5 variations of this axiom)), Cardinality (e.g. Countable and Uncountable sets, Cantor's diagonalization, Taski's Theorem, Schroder-Berstein Theorem), Defining Rational and Real numbers (Dedekind cuts, supremum and infimum).

Probability Theory: Lebesgue Measure (Not heavily covered but it turns out to be very important in a hw), Measurable Space and Probability Space, Conditional Probability, Bayes Theorem, Independence, Random Variable, Linearity, Expectation, Stochastic Process and Markov Chain (Definition and representation in graphs especially having infinite walks in a graph), Reachability event, Buchi set, . (There are other topics Amir didn't taught in class like Variance and the distributions but they are required.)

Game Theory: Combinatorial Games (Nim with 2,3 and n heaps), solve games using Topological ordering in a DAG (Directed Acyclic Graph) and XOR operations, Sprague-Grundy Theorem, One shot games (Prisoner's Dilemma, Pollution game, Battle of sexes, Matching Pennies), Pure Nash Equilibrium (Using Dominant strategy to solve game), Mixed Nash Equilibrium (Using Probabilistic strategy to solve games), Nash Theorem, Arena, Reachability game (similar to Markov Chain), Buchi game.

There are also other topics that have connections to this course some are required some don't. (Control Theory, Group Theory, Measure Theory, Linear temporal logic, Ordinality of Set Theory, )

There are a lot of books to read. And most of them are not friendly to beginners at all. There are alternatives but these books goes deeper than any books I found in the library. Of course you go look at the "handbook of Discrete Math" but it doesn't really give you strong idea of why this or that theorem is true. So you still need to read the books.
I like Chen and Koh book.

Discrete and Combinatorial Mathematics by Grimaldi
Concrete Mathematics by Knuth et al
Principles and Techniques in Combinatorics by Chen and Koh
A First Course in Probability by Ross
Elementary Number Theory by Burton
Set Theory: A First Course by Cunningham
Introduction to Graph Theory by West
Algorithmic Game Theory by Nisan and Roughgarden
Problem Solving Strategies by Engel
102 Combinatorial Problems by Andreescu and Feng
Winning Ways for Your Mathematical Plays by Berlekamp et al

There are also some videos TAs or Amir might share but I recommend watching more that are not provided by them since a lot of the topics / questions really require guides or clarification. There are a lot of online resources related to this course so use them wisely! There is too many channels I can recommend and there are a lot of the topic this course has covered. So I recommend watch the videos in categories (Like watch videos about proofs like MIT courses, organic chemistry(Probably the weakest channel with proofs but very basic), TrevTutor, Math Sorcerer.)

If you have read this far, here is a reward to you. His website to his course with the recordings. https://amir.goharshady.com/teaching/honors-discrete-math
comment_teaching:
Amir is a good and fun teacher. However, the pacing is really fast. And don't expect the notes by Amir and TAs to be detailed. (He is pretty lazy when it comes to writing notes but he wrote so much in problem sets/homework/exam questions idk why lol.) TAs simply just copy Amir notes and take some words from him in the recording. So, make sure to take notes as well. I had to rewatch lecture notes and read chapters on books multiple times to get a grasp on a single content. Also for those who don't know, all tutorials sections are just additional lectures for students to attend and they are mandatory. So if you miss one lecture, you are in a disadvantage unless you have a very good background on Discrete Math before hand. I highly recommend you guys to ask TAs and Amir for help. They are friendly. But make sure to book the meetings asap.

TAs are good. I had problems from listening to one of the TAs since he don't speak English well. But I still think he is a decent TA. Since he patiently answers your questions. The TA tutor sections are also very crucial for the course. So attend them as well. If not watch the recording.
comment_workload:
This course has ridiculous workload. Not only you have lectures that you cannot skip. You have extra exercises that are required to do. Especially counting problems. In week 2, Amir have given us over 400 problems and some of them are advanced questions involving group theory. This is impossible to do and I only managed to do 1/4 of it from sacrificing my daily time and skipping lectures.

There are totally 12 problem sets with 10 problems each. They are not required but very highly recommended. The problem set are usually hard, there are easy ones. But when it comes to counting/principle of inclusion and exclusion/pigeonhole principle/number theory, Amir loves to add Olympiad questions to it and they are generally very hard. I believe the TAs just copy the solutions in the internet as their solutions. (And how they find the observation to solution is really unclear.). So expect to spent a long time to finish these questions as well. I didn't do problem sets after set 7 since I fumbled other courses while mainly handling this course.

The homework at the beginning is pretty easy as long as you do some combinatorics questions. After that it is not easy. So you might spent a few days if not weeks to solve 2-3 questions. Btw, student can only submit their hw via latex, so if you have no knowledge on it, have fun spending extra time on it. (I stared at the screen for days. Always thinking the solutions while eating, walking, laying on bed or taking a bath. It took me a long time to get them right.) Don't forget, there are questions that you need to learn how to answer in the internet as Amir didn't taught them much. You need to give correctness on your algorithm, proving it is correct. Read/watch Measure Theory and Markov Decision Process.

There are many books you can read but they are high recommended again (The books are in the contents part since they are pretty much compulsory). But Amir also shares videos or extra reading materials. The videos are usually long but you can skip them if you want to. I watched and read all of them.

Oh yes, we cannot forget about the study part even though we did a lot already. There are a few ways you can study. Either you watch videos relate to it or watch other courses taught by Amir. It is very easy to see the similaries and there are exam papers publish publicly there. (No idea why no sample papers for this course. smh).

For those who dislike the tremendous workload like this, don't take this course. Although Amir is no longer teaching in UST, he is still a great teacher. He just likes to make things 100x harder.
comment_grading:
Homework 40%
Mid Term Exam 30%
Final Exam 30%

The homework policy is quite generous in general, you are allowed to submit twices for each homework. After the first submission, you get feedback from TAs or Amir and then resubmit it. The score for each homework is the maximum of two attempts.

As for the homework difficulty, it is generally hard after homework 1.
HW1: 10/10
HW2: 8.6/10
HW3: 10/10
HW4: 10/10

HW4 is where you need to understand Measure Theory and there is literally nothing you can do except googling.

As for the mid term, well it was pretty devastating. Even though it is easier than weekly problem sets, it is generally hard. The mid term covered EVERYTHING before the the date of the mid term exam, that means the lectures for that week also counts into it. Also, the weekly problem sets solutions are quite late to come out. I understand that TAs are very busy but I wish at least there are guides on how to do these type of questions OR just give us a sample paper like they did in 3711H.

As for my score, I got 6/30, my friend had 8/30. (Yes, I am terrible. I am sure most of the students here have higher score than me. I had mental breakdown before I had the grade and asked TA for help to save me from failing the course.)

As for the final exam, while it is easier, it turns out to have question on calculating which is unexpected. I was expecting something similar to the problem sets. So I didn't score perfectly on that part. Same goes for Combinatorics. Other than that, it was not that bad. I got 16.5/30. Much much better than mid term.

I got A- in the end. THANK YOU AMIR YOU ARE THE BEST!!!

comment_content:
[Some words after watching Amir's offering]

Apparently Amir's version is way more all-rounded than the usual offering, in terms of the depth and breadth of course content. In the typical version, the teaching sequence is

Propositional logic -> Number theory -> Combinatorics -> Probability Theory -> (Optional) Graph theory

But Amir's version has the sequence of

Propositional logic + Induction (a part of Peano's axiom) -> Combinatorics -> Graph theory + Graph algos -> Number theory + Cryptography -> Axiom of completeness -> Probability -> Stochastic process -> Game theory

If 3711H is "a feast for algo geeks", then 2711H shall be "a fine-dining for algebraists, analysts and algo geeks" since the non-graph-theoretic topics are really foundations for mathematicians living on each side, while the graph theory topics can be treated as a taster of 3711H since the related core topics are covered with some proof of correctness, but without the important C++ implementations and runtime analysis.

Initially I didn't understand why graph theory was introduced at the beginning of the course. After covering topics like Markov chains and game theory, I realized that there might be no better teaching sequences for putting graph theory elsewhere.

If you wish to go further based on the course content, the following courses are highly recommended -

(i). The Honors analysis sequence (1023 up to 3043) will help you continue completing the "axiom of completeness", as well as why measure theory and Lebesgue integration theory are necessary in modern analysis. I would say solely based on 2711H, students may feel difficult to see why Lebesgue integral is important and necessary.

(ii) The discussion on elementary number theory is just telling one fundamental fact that "\mathbb Z" is a Unique Factorization Domain (UFD)". The Honors (and graduate-level) algebra courses (3131 + 5111) will explore more algebraic structures and similar properties; Also many modern crypto algos are based on commutative algebra and algebraic geometry.

(iii) The measure-theoretic probability (2431 + 5411) courses will make use of what you learnt in the Honors analysis courses and rebuild every concepts in classical probability. Without such foundations, the famous central limit theorem and the laws of large numbers (and related variants) cannot be understood rigorously.

(iv) For the graph-theoretical topics, indeed 3711H is the best continuation.

Like 3711H, I admire the hard efforts made by Amir and his teaching team in this course.

[End]

1. Official textbook: Notes on the course website, and his own lecture - The content taught in class might be different from that in the notes, and not all stuff is taught.

2. Course structure:

========Part One: Pure Logic stuff=======

TL;DR: Literally basic building block in math.

i. Propositional logic, inference,... (Most useful takeaway: p implies q iff not q implies not p - A logical mistake that everyone makes in daily life)
ii. Set and cardinality
iii. Function (Mapping between sets), and injective, surjective, bijective...

==========Part Two: Number Theoty===========

TL;DR: Basic number theory.

iv. Mod arithmetic and number theory (Euclid's algorithm, Fermat's little theorem, C.R.T... - One shall study abstract algebra topics like group and ring for further study)
v. Cryptography (RSA)

========Part Three: Counting principle==========

TL;DR: One of the hardest types of question appears here! You have to prove identity, usually combinatorial approach instead of analytic. It is quite difficult because you may not figure out the correct scenario for the proof!

vi. Permutation and combination, binomial coefficient
vii. Inclusion-exclusion principle
viii. Analysis of algorithm (Big-Oh, Big-Theta, Big-Omega...)

=========Part Four: Probability======

TL;DR: Discrete random variables. The only continuous stuff taught is normal distribution.

ix. Random variable
x. Expectation and variance, conditional probability
xi. Special distribution (Bernoulli, binomial, geometric, normal...)

=========Part Five: Others=======

xii. Strong/weak induction
xiii. Recursion
xiv. Graph theory

comment_teaching: Sunil's teaching is top notch (just like desmond!!!).  He stresses intuition and understanding over memorization which is really great.  He also enjoys teaching a lot which can be seen by his excited voice when he talks about certain theorems lol.  This semester, classes were at 9am but I attended every single one just because of Sunil.
comment_workload:
Moderate workload.

comment_teaching:
His teaching is logical and clear.
Every topic is explained from easy concepts, then slowly developed into complicated ones.
He is also willing to answer any questions patiently.
It is worth it to take the H course.

TAs are willing to answer questions.
Tutorials covers some questions in the question banks provided.
comment_workload: I need to work a few hours for around 2 to 3 days for every assignment and spend 2 to 3 days for revision but still feeling not enough before each test. Although it may because lectures are in the morning and I had a hard time not to fall at sleep so that I need more time to understand topics that I did not concentrate and listen, some complicated concepts needs time to be understand fully. (there are videos for the lecture to review or just not physically attend the lecture but still I prefer attending them in person) As a year 1 student I have not adapt to a good study habit for university yet, if I had done what the professor said, which is to preview each topic before lecture, it may be much better.
comment_grading:
It is good in my perspective as above mean gets A-.
My score
Assignments 10% 16/20,17/20,20/20,18/20
Midterm 40% 80/100
Final 50% 90/100

The mean is around 17,18 for assignments,
around 82 for midterm and
around 84 for final.

comment_content:
The content includes is as following:
- logic and methods of proof. ie. proof by induction.
- some number theory ie. RSA, euler totient theorem, Chinese remainder theorem.
- algorithm analysis (Big O, theta, omega notation and asympotic)
- discrete probability (some basic stuff)

Overall the content are not twoo difficult but is definitely interesting.
comment_teaching:
Prof. Sunil is a decent teacher. You can tell that he is passinate about his topics and would like to engage the class. He is very nice when asnwering any of our questions.
However, he would skip some of the details (such as some proofs) in the lecture and reading the lecture notes would help. His handwriting is sometime hard to recognize.
Some of the harder problems are in the tutorials so I would recommand at least attempt some of the questions in the question bank.
comment_workload: Workload is fine. There are 4 homeworks in total and each one took about 2 hrs to finish in about a week. It is quite time consuming, especially when sometimes stuck on some problems, over I guess its fine when spreading in the whole week.
comment_grading:
The grading is very good. I did not do so well at the midterm and final (around 85) and still get an A-.
I would recommand this course for getting a good grade.

comment_content:
The content of this course is very comprehensive to me. It covers a great range of topics like Logic, Number theory, Probability, Recursion, etc.
It can be a bit tricky for beginners like me. I've put quite a lot of effort into this course to understand those concepts and theories, but I have to say I'm fully satisfied with this course's content.
comment_teaching: Prof. Sunil's teaching method is great. He will guide you to think about how the problem comes out and how we develop a solution to it step by step.
comment_workload:
5 assignments, each with 8-10 questions
midterm + final

Almost all the questions in assignments and exams are proof questions, which requires you to memorize those theories and use them in the right way. They are not unreasonably difficult to do, as long as you are familiar with those several question patterns. But it did take me quite a long time to memorize theories and practice proving.
I have to spend at least 5 hours on each assignment. TA also provides supplementary questions of each chapter for revision.
Exam questions are not as tricky as assignment questions.
comment_grading:
I heard that overall the grading scheme is very good.

5 assignments: 19/20, 20/20, 19/20, 20/20, -/- (the last one was not marked)
midterm: 100/100
final: 100/100
Got A+
